speaker: interviewer
text: with Chana Gitalis, May 26, 1995. This is tape three. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You were telling us that you left your little home that you made with this other family, and you moved to Lutsk. Why would you go to Lutsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my brother-in-law was in Lutsk. They took him away. And they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who took him away?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the Russian people. They took him away for work. When they came in, they took away the men. So they took away my hus-- my brother-in-law, and he was in Lutsk. We didn't know before, but they told us. He gave us-- he sent us a message with somebody. He is in Lutsk, and if we can come, he will be very happy.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I left my sister there, and I went by myself to Lutsk. It wasn't so easy to go. So I went with the Russian soldiers. They took me to Lutsk. How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: By-- they had cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: Truck?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, truck. They went to Lutsk, so they took my [INAUDIBLE] and they took me there. And I find my brother-in-law, and he said, you know, I want you-- you and my sister to come there so we'll be together. So we find a place-- a house. The house was-- was without windows, without doors, but we didn't have nothing else.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the next day, I went back with the soldiers, and my sister packed the beautiful things we had, [LAUGHS] and we went to Lutsk. We came to Lutsk. We didn't have anything to eat, nothing. So my brother-in-law, they send him to Harkov. So in Harkov, he bought thread. Thread was a very expensive thing in-- in Russia there. So she went once-- she went on the market and she sold the thread.

speaker: interviewee
text: But in Russia, you-- you can't-- you can't sell and take money. It's not allowed. So the sol-- the-- one policeman took her away, and she got-- and they took her to jail. My sister-- my sister was so sick. She was two months in jail. It was very hard for me until I got the papers and took her out from jail. And then there--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did it take to get her out of jail?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- one second, a Russian man came in into the-- into the house, and he want to see what I-- what things I have, how rich we are. So he came in and he saw nothing. So I sa-- I said to him-- and I start to cry-- please help me. My sister is sick, and you see what I have. I have no money, nothing. Without her, I'll die. And we got through so much. I didn't let him go out. I beg him. I-- I cried terrible. So the next day, he took-- he brought my sister. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But she was high-- she was in the-- Lutsk, it was the biggest the jail in the city. She was there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you manage to pay off that lady for the sewing machine? Did you have the sewing machine with you when you went to Lutsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you left, you left it there.

speaker: interviewee
text: We left everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any other events that happened when you were in Lutsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a-- it was a big city, but not much Jewish people, and life was very hard. We couldn't make a living. But we remained in the-- there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother-in-law-- from my brother-law, what he-- he did. We-- we had something to eat. The main thing, we had something to eat, and we lived very poor. And one day, a man came in and he find out we are alive. And they brought me a paper, like a card, written in my name, from Russia. He gave me-- I don't know he said but I didn't want to tell me. It was my-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my-- how do you say [NON-ENGLISH]? My [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your fiance.

speaker: interviewee
text: My fiance.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your husband-to-be.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and I was very happy. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he started to write letters to me, and he'll be-- in one year-- it took one year he came. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: He came to you in Lutsk?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not in Lutsk. My sister went to Luboml, and we stayed there-- she stayed there for a while with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: You returned to Luboml? [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: To Luboml, yeah. From Luboml--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel when you went back to Luboml?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't-- how would you feel? We felt-- we felt very, very, bad. Every, every--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go back to your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No home, they took away our home. Just the garden was-- I told you. I-- I-- I'd been once in my-- in my-- in my garden. We had big plums every year. So the plums were green, so I took the plums. And-- and I start to cry, and I watered the plums with my-- with my tears, I watered the plums from our garden. That's what I did. And then I went back in a pl-- we had a-- a place. My sister went first to Hel, because we could go away from Russia. We want to go away. So she went to-- before with her hu-- with her boyfriend, and I was waiting for my husband, for my husband-- for my [NON-ENGLISH] to come back. And then we went to Hel, and we were married in Hel.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hel.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1945 we were married. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then we stayed in Hel not long, because the Jewish people left. The-- the-- the Polish people wasn't so great to us. Some people--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give me an example of how you were treated?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some people got killed after the war-- after the war in Hel, so we were afraid to stay there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just like this, they didn't like us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we went to Germany. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The place in Germany was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I forgot the name. A lot of little places. We couldn't find a place. But in one place we came, they took us away. We went to Berlin, and from Berlin we went to the-- to the-- to the place we've been for-- for four years there. And the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which was which?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the-- I-- I'll show you before. In Zeilsheim. It's not far from Frankfurt am Main.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is Zeilsheim?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a-- the American. They made the-- the places for the Jewish people. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you say you stayed there for four years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. Yeah. My son was born there in 1946, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and we remained there until we got to Canada. We couldn't go before.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do during those four years?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a lot of people. We had activities. Nice, very nice. it was a school there. It was like a city, but we lived very happily. Very happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you find out what happened to other members of your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: We find out right away they are not alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened with your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I find my brother. I was-- when I was in [INAUDIBLE], so right after-- after the war, I came to [INAUDIBLE], and at my friend's house, came-- because the house was on the way where the soldiers used to go. It was the-- the place they went in. They had something to drink, to eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was there, and soldiers came in. And they were sitting there. And they have tea and everything. I-- I came just from-- I was-- I-- I was two days for-- I came from-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from Lutsk. And they were sitting, so I said, you know what? My brother is in the Polish army, so-- and his name is Samuel-- Melamed. So they start to laugh. [LAUGHS] I said-- so he said again, what is your brother's name? I said, Sam Melamed. So he took out a picture. He was a-- he was a corporal. I don't know. He had three-- three stars. Corporal? Sergeant?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, three stars. So he said, look at this. Do you recognize this one? So, yes, I said, 'That's my brother.' So I start to cry. They all-- they all cried with me. I saw-- I saw my brother. So I said to them, you know what, I didn't see my brother, and if he's alive and you know him, send me my brother. I want to see him. In three days, my brother came. And I still was there in the house. And-- and it was cold winter. And the sol-- there were soldiers, they came just because it's cold. They came to sit a little bit there. And my brother opened the door, and came in. And we-- we saw each other, and-- and we cried. So everyone start to cry when they saw us together. It was-- I-- I can't forget. I will always remember when I saw my brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel when you saw him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't-- I can't explain you. I can't describe you how I felt. We were so happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he join you afterwards in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Then he went back to the army, with the-- and-- and maybe a-- one year later, he find us again, because we-- we lost each other again. But we were happy. We-- we were alive, he find us, and we were-- we've been together. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And a few years later, I had a cousin-- a second cousin here in Toronto. So my brother came the first, and then he sent us papers. We came here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was your cousin?

speaker: interviewee
text: My co-- he is not alive also. His name was Avrum Silverberg. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: He helped you come to Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you arrive in Canada?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to Canada in 1951, in June-- in May. It was the end of May.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go? Which city?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went-- we came to Toronto. We stayed there for three months, because my husband has, in Montreal, relatives. So he didn't want to stay in Toronto. We went to-- to Montreal, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and we lived there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now I came back to Toronto because my children are here. My husband died, so I came here.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did your husband pass away?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] 13 years ago. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So my children didn't want me to stay in Toron-- in Montreal, so I came here and remain here. And I have nice children, nice grandchildren. They're very good to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many grandchildren do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have five. One is just now born, two and a half weeks ago. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever think about your experiences from the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: My experiences from the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think about them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very often. They are in me. [LAUGHS] We can't forget things like this we got through.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have dreams or nightmares about those experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much. A lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe some of those dreams or nightmares?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes I have a dream I'm home. I talk to my mother. I see my home. I see with the garden. I see my neighbors. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Very much. But things you can't forget. It's in you.

speaker: interviewer
text: How has your experiences affected you in life?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll try to-- to get-- to get away from it, always. I'm strong, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have your experiences affected the way you've brought up your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: I brought up my children the way-- like home. They went to school, to Hebrew school, all of them. And I tried the best of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: I survived? I don't know why. It's a miracle. Lots of times I said to myself, did I survive? It's true? I-- I can't believe it [INAUDIBLE]. There are days I can't believe it, but I got through.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes when I have a dream in-- like sometimes I saw the German sol-- soldiers. We ran with the things on our-- on our-- little things which we took. And how could we-- how can I live now, since such a bad time I had? I myself ask. This is a miracle. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Why did you want to give us this interview?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you ask?

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you want to tell your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: Really, I never told my children nothing. They asked me. I-- if I start to tell them, I didn't sleep for nights. Because when I say something to you, I tell you, I see everything. When I tell you something, I see everything with my eyes, like I-- I can touch it. So I never want to start. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you called me. I didn't want to, really. But now I see I'm happy because of my children. They should see. Maybe my grandchildren. Sometimes when they see something, they'll say, my grandmother did it. She survived, and she's strong. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And my children are very good [INAUDIBLE]. Very nice, good children. I'm happy to see them. On Saturday, sometimes, I make a nice-- Friday, they make Kiddish, and I see the [INAUDIBLE]. [? It can be ?]-- I'm so happy, I can't describe you. No words. This is my whole life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have a special message that you'd like to give your children or the rest of the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: The message is the Jewish people should live in-- in peace. No more-- no more wars like we had. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is my wish, and my grandchildren and grandchildren shouldn't have this, what I got through.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to thank you very much, Mrs. Gitalis, for letting us into your home and your heart.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I understand that you want to share a song that you wrote when you were hiding for those two years.

speaker: interviewee
text: If you want to. My-- my voice is not so special.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you share that song with us?

speaker: interviewee
text: You want me to sing now?

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll take a break and you can--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell us briefly what the song is about?

speaker: interviewee
text: The song is about hunger. We had been very hungry, and we-- so I have the song.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the name of the song?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of the song is 'A Jail Without Bars.' [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm going to sing in-- in Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. [SINGING IN POLISH] This is the song.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lovely. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not much. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture taken-- 1927. On the right is-- one of the right is my sister, Hinda. The second is a cousin, and the third one is my aunt. And there is my grandfather and grandmother, and on the knee is me, myself?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us the name of the-- of my aunt and your grand--

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of my aunt is Faigy. The name of my grandmother is [? Riva, ?] and my grandfather is [? Zella, ?] and on the knee is Chana. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a picture from 1937. It's my family in our garden, on a hammock. The right-- the right one is me, Chana. And the other-- the other side is Esther, my sister, and my father and mother, Elka and Leibisz. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And this is a picture from 1938. It-- my-- my brother-in-law and sister. My brother-in-law, Aaron, my sister, Hinda, and the two children, Ella and Schmelik. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is my brother, Samuel Melamed, and-- with his friend in the Polish army.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewer
text: And which one is your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the right side-- the right. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is my husband, Symcha Gitalis, in the Polish army in 1938. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is my engagement picture, Symcha and Chana Gitalis, like Chana and Symcha Gitalis when we met. [LAUGHS] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is the man who hide me in [INAUDIBLE]. His name is Ge-- Gerasimuk. Tmosz Gerasimuk. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is my-- my aunt in [INAUDIBLE], which we find her, and she took care of us, me and my sister. Faigy-- her name was Faigy. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a place in Zeilsheim-- Zeilsheim, Germany, when we were with those children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure, after the war. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is a picture when we arrived to the camp the first time, me and my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zeilsheim.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not far from Frankfurt am Main. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is a picture for me and my husband, [INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] This is a picture taken in Vancouver with my son, five years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the name of your son?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zeke. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] This is a picture taken in-- in New York. It's Norman-- my nephew, Norman, and his wife and their daughter. Norman Fisher. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] This is items I took from my home. I cherish always with me, and it's a knife. It says Shabbat [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son, Abie. This is my-- my other son, Simon. This is my granddaughter, Sharon, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and my grandchild, Zev, the oldest, Sharna, and the third one is not here. Too bad. His name is Joshua. And this is my grandchild, Noah, and the little one, Efrem. I remembered the name. [LAUGHTER] [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] So, you want to say? I don't know,

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you want-- hold it for a second. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is my mother, Chana, and we heard that you're doing this special thing, so we came after to see what's going on here. And we're very proud of her and very happy that she survived the ordeal that she went through. And we're really happy that you put it on tape for us, because it's very difficult to get the stories from her. And some things that I might know, my brother, Abie, doesn't know, or some of the grandkids don't know. And I think that, as a third party, you might have really stimulated her memory. And we're really proud of her, and we're happy that we have her story intact now, on a copy of this tape. We love her very much, and we hope that she's with us for a lot longer, because she has her grandchildren to see get married [LAUGHTER] and [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: You'll be around. OK. That's all I have to say.

speaker: interviewee
text: Are you going to introduce us all?
